title: Dilution of the Cell Suspension
description: {"blocks":[{"key":"alh8g","text":"This protocol outlines the requirements for successful dilution of cell suspension.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}}],"entityMap":{}}
guidelines: {"blocks":[{"key":"8pgtr","text":"Hematocytometer (Hausser Bright-Line, catalog number 3500) ","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"bold","offset":0,"length":15}],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"13ql2","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"e1vvl","text":"Cell Depth: 0.100 mm +/- 2% (1/10 mm) ","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"dl9po","text":"Volume: 0.1 µl per large square (red) ","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"ddca7","text":"Ruling Pattern: Improved Neubauer, 1/400 Square mm ","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"9q57b","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"7svdi","text":"Rulings cover 9 square millimeters. Boundary lines of the Neubauer ruling are the center lines of the groups of three. (These are indicated in the illustration below.) The central square millimeter is ruled into 25 groups of 16 small squares, each group separated by triple lines, the middle one of which is the boundary. The ruled surface is 0.10 mm below the cover glass, so that the volume over each of the 16 small squares is .00025 cubic steps: Re-suspend the cell pellet in 500 µL, trituration solution .
Take 10 µL of the suspension and dilute it in 10 µL, Trypan Blue Solution (Gibco 15250061) .
Take 10 µL of this mix to count on the hematocytometer.
Count the living cells and also the number of dead cells (colored in blue-black, the number should be less than 20% of the total number of cells).

V \text { to add }=\left[\left(\frac{n b \text { of cells counted } \times \text { dilution factor}}{\text {dial volume } \times \text { seeding concentration}}\right) \times \text { cell suspension volume }\right]-\text { cell suspension volume } The volume of solution covering 1 millimeter squared of the hematocytometer (see the "How to Count the Cells" section) is 0.1 µl and the dilution factor is 2. If the concentration required for seeding is 240,000 cells/ml, the volume of trituration solution to be added to the 490 µl of cell suspension is: V \text { to add }=\left[\left(\frac{n b \text { of cells counted } \times 2}{0.0001 \mathrm{ml} \times 240 \text { 000 cells } / \mathrm{ml}}\right) \times 0.490 \mathrm{ml}\right]-0.490 \mathrm{ml}
To count your cell sample, dilute 10 µL, of your sample in 10 µL, Trypan Blue Solution (Gibco 15250061) and then load the hematocytometer.

The counting chamber is covered with a laser engraved grid. Quadrant lines make counting easier. Choose the appropriate quadrant size based on the density of the cells in your sample. For example, for a small number of cells (less than 20 in a quadrant of 16), count all the cells in the 4 quadrants of 16, and divide this number by 4. For samples with a quantity of more than 20 cells, choose the diagonal in the 4 quadrants of 16.
Regardless of the area you choose or the density of the sample, count at least 20 to 50 cells per quadrant.

Not all cells will fall perfectly into the quadrants. For example, you can count the cells that touch the top and left lines but ignore the cells that touch the bottom and right lines. Use a cell counter to keep track of the number of cells counted.

The volume of solution covering 1 millimeter squared of the hematocytometer is 0.1 µl and the dilution factor is 2. If the concentration required for seeding is 240,000 cells/ml, the volume of trituration solution to be added to the 490 µl of cell suspension is: V \text { to add }=\left[\left(\frac{n b \text { of cells counted } \times \text { dilution factor}}{\text {dial volume } \times \text { seeding concentration}}\right) \times \text { cell suspension volume }\right]-\text { cell suspension volume }

While counting cells under the microscope, assess the viability of the cells in your sample. Trypan blue is a dye used to determine viability. The living cell excludes this dye, but the dead cell has no intact membrane allowing the trypan blue to pass through and mark the cytoplasm. In phase contrast, living cells appear bright and golden and dead cells appear dull and blue. The number of dead cells should be less than 20% of the total number of cells.
When you have finished counting, clean the counting chamber.
creator: Nicolas Giguère
